structured.summary: This paper proposes COCO-LM, a self-supervised pretraining framework for language models that combines corrective language modeling (CLM) and sequence contrastive learning (SCL) on corrupted text sequences generated by an auxiliary model. COCO-LM improves both pretraining efficiency and downstream task performance compared to state-of-the-art models like ELECTRA and RoBERTa.

strengths.point: Novel combination of token-level corrective language modeling and sequence-level contrastive learning to improve language model pretraining.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.3 describes the two new pretraining tasks, CLM and SCL, and how they complement each other to enhance token-level semantics and sequence representation uniformity.

strengths.point: Significant improvements in pretraining efficiency, achieving comparable or better accuracy with substantially fewer GPU hours.
strengths.evidence: Section 5.1 and Figure 3 show COCO-LM matches ELECTRA's MNLI accuracy with only 50% of its pretraining GPU hours and outperforms RoBERTa and ELECTRA with the same compute.

strengths.point: Strong empirical results on standard benchmarks (GLUE and SQuAD) demonstrating improved accuracy and parameter efficiency.
strengths.evidence: Table 1 and Section 5.1 report COCO-LM outperforming recent state-of-the-art models on GLUE average scores and matching Megatron 3.9B accuracy with less than 10% of its parameters.

strengths.point: Comprehensive ablation studies and analyses that validate the contributions of both CLM and SCL components.
strengths.evidence: Section 5.1 and 5.2 provide detailed ablations showing the individual and combined benefits of CLM and SCL, as well as analyses on representation alignment and uniformity.

weaknesses.point: The auxiliary model training is independent and not influenced by the main model, which may limit optimality of corrupted sequences.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 6 discusses this limitation and suggests future work to better understand and automate auxiliary model training.

weaknesses.point: Contrastive pairs are constructed using relatively simple augmentations (cropping and MLM replacements), potentially limiting the robustness of learned representations.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 6 acknowledges this limitation and proposes exploring more advanced data augmentation techniques for contrastive learning.

weaknesses.point: Lack of reported error bars or statistical significance testing for experimental results.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4 mentions reporting median/average results but does not include error bars or confidence intervals, which could affect the reliability of comparisons.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not provide detailed analysis on the computational overhead introduced by the auxiliary model and contrastive learning during pretraining.
weaknesses.evidence: While Figure 3 shows overall GPU hour efficiency, the specific costs of auxiliary model training and SCL are not separately quantified.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on potential negative societal impacts beyond training cost and environmental concerns.
weaknesses.evidence: Checklist mentions discussion on negative impacts in Appendix F, but the main text lacks elaboration on ethical considerations or misuse risks.

weaknesses.point: The method is evaluated primarily on English benchmarks; generalization to other languages or domains is not studied.
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments focus on GLUE and SQuAD datasets without mention of multilingual or domain adaptation evaluations.

weaknesses.point: The auxiliary model configuration choices (e.g., disabling dropout, shallow layers) are empirically motivated but lack theoretical justification.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.3 and 5.1 describe these design choices but do not provide deeper analysis or ablation on why these configurations are optimal.

questions.question: Have the authors considered or experimented with more sophisticated data augmentations for constructing contrastive pairs beyond cropping and MLM replacements?
questions.motivation: More diverse augmentations could further improve representation robustness and downstream performance.

questions.question: Can the authors provide more detailed profiling of the computational overhead introduced by the auxiliary model and sequence contrastive learning during pretraining?
questions.motivation: Understanding the cost breakdown is important for assessing practical deployment and scalability.

questions.question: How does COCO-LM perform on languages other than English or on domain-specific corpora? Have the authors tested its generalizability?
questions.motivation: Evaluating cross-lingual and domain robustness would strengthen the applicability claims of the method.

questions.question: Is it possible to jointly train or adapt the auxiliary model based on feedback from the main model to optimize the corruption process?
questions.motivation: Such joint training could potentially yield more informative corrupted sequences and improve pretraining effectiveness.

critique_points.point_001: The auxiliary model training is independent and not influenced by the main model, which may limit optimality of corrupted sequences. (Section 6 discusses this limitation and suggests future work to better understand and automate auxiliary model training.)
critique_points.point_002: Contrastive pairs are constructed using relatively simple augmentations (cropping and MLM replacements), potentially limiting the robustness of learned representations. (Section 6 acknowledges this limitation and proposes exploring more advanced data augmentation techniques for contrastive learning.)
critique_points.point_003: Lack of reported error bars or statistical significance testing for experimental results. (Section 4 mentions reporting median/average results but does not include error bars or confidence intervals, which could affect the reliability of comparisons.)
critique_points.point_004: The paper does not provide detailed analysis on the computational overhead introduced by the auxiliary model and contrastive learning during pretraining. (While Figure 3 shows overall GPU hour efficiency, the specific costs of auxiliary model training and SCL are not separately quantified.)
critique_points.point_005: Limited discussion on potential negative societal impacts beyond training cost and environmental concerns. (Checklist mentions discussion on negative impacts in Appendix F, but the main text lacks elaboration on ethical considerations or misuse risks.)
critique_points.point_006: The method is evaluated primarily on English benchmarks; generalization to other languages or domains is not studied. (Experiments focus on GLUE and SQuAD datasets without mention of multilingual or domain adaptation evaluations.)
critique_points.point_007: The auxiliary model configuration choices (e.g., disabling dropout, shallow layers) are empirically motivated but lack theoretical justification. (Section 3.3 and 5.1 describe these design choices but do not provide deeper analysis or ablation on why these configurations are optimal.)
critique_points.point_008: Have the authors considered or experimented with more sophisticated data augmentations for constructing contrastive pairs beyond cropping and MLM replacements? (More diverse augmentations could further improve representation robustness and downstream performance.)
critique_points.point_009: Can the authors provide more detailed profiling of the computational overhead introduced by the auxiliary model and sequence contrastive learning during pretraining? (Understanding the cost breakdown is important for assessing practical deployment and scalability.)
critique_points.point_010: How does COCO-LM perform on languages other than English or on domain-specific corpora? Have the authors tested its generalizability? (Evaluating cross-lingual and domain robustness would strengthen the applicability claims of the method.)
critique_points.point_011: Is it possible to jointly train or adapt the auxiliary model based on feedback from the main model to optimize the corruption process? (Such joint training could potentially yield more informative corrupted sequences and improve pretraining effectiveness.)
